fragments.content: Type 2 diabetes affects over 537 million adults globally, projected to reach 783 million by 2045. Prediabetes (impaired fasting glucose or impaired glucose tolerance) affects 1 in 3 US adults, with 15-30% progressing to diabetes within 5 years without intervention. Diabetes prevention targets modifiable risk factors: obesity (especially central adiposity), physical inactivity, unhealthy diet, and social determinants. The Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP) landmark trial demonstrated 58% risk reduction through lifestyle intervention (7% weight loss, 150 min/week activity) versus 31% with metformin. Prevention strategies include primary prevention (preventing onset in at-risk individuals), secondary prevention (early detection through screening), and tertiary prevention (complication prevention in diagnosed patients). Social-ecological models recognize individual behavior occurs within environmental contexts requiring multi-level interventions.

Evidence-based diabetes prevention approaches: (1) Lifestyle programs—CDC-recognized National DPP delivering year-long curriculum on nutrition, physical activity, stress management, problem-solving; delivered in-person, online, or distance learning; (2) Pharmacotherapy—metformin for high-risk individuals (BMI≥35, age<60, history of gestational diabetes), GLP-1 agonists showing promise for weight loss/diabetes prevention; (3) Policy interventions—sugar-sweetened beverage taxes, healthy school food standards, built environment for active living, food labeling requirements; (4) Healthcare system integration—routine prediabetes screening, electronic health record prompts, referral systems to DPP, insurance coverage for prevention programs; (5) Community approaches—faith-based programs, community health worker delivery, culturally adapted interventions; (6) Digital health—mobile apps, remote coaching, wearable devices. Cost-effectiveness analyses show lifestyle intervention cost-effective long-term. Scalability remains challenge requiring innovative delivery models.

Diabetes prevention assessment includes: (1) Screening criteria—USPSTF recommends screening adults 35-70 years with overweight/obesity; ADA recommends screening all adults ≥35 years, earlier with risk factors (family history, race/ethnicity, gestational diabetes history, PCOS, hypertension, dyslipidemia); (2) Diagnostic tests—fasting plasma glucose (100-125 mg/dL prediabetes, ≥126 diabetes), HbA1c (5.7-6.4% prediabetes, ≥6.5% diabetes), 2-hour OGTT (140-199 mg/dL prediabetes, ≥200 diabetes); (3) Risk stratification—ADA Diabetes Risk Test, FINDRISC, QDiabetes risk scores identifying candidates for intensive intervention; (4) Monitoring—annual screening for prediabetes progression, weight tracking, lifestyle behavior assessment; (5) Population surveillance—NHANES prevalence data, BRFSS behavioral indicators, national diabetes registries. Continuous glucose monitoring emerging for prediabetes research. Limitations include HbA1c variability by race/ethnicity, anemia affecting accuracy, and discordance between diagnostic tests.

source: CDC National Diabetes Prevention Program, ADA Standards of Care, Diabetes Prevention Program Outcomes Study, WHO Global Diabetes Compact
